title: Legacy mint accounting overissues cTokens for fee-on-transfer deposits
claim: The legacy flattened CToken mints against the user-supplied `mintAmount`, but its abstract `doTransferIn` hook only returns an error code and cannot report how much underlying was actually received. A fee-on-transfer or deflationary underlying therefore lets the market mint cTokens as if the full amount arrived even when less cash was credited.
impact: If such an underlying is listed through this legacy implementation, an attacker can mint excess cTokens against underfunded deposits and later redeem or borrow against claims that are not backed by real cash, diluting suppliers and potentially draining the market.
paths: mint -> mintFresh computes `mintTokens` from `mintAmount` -> `doTransferIn(minter, mintAmount)` receives less than `mintAmount` -> attacker receives cTokens backed by insufficient cash

title: Legacy repay path clears debt using the nominal amount instead of cash actually received
claim: In the legacy flattened CToken, `repayBorrowFresh` subtracts `vars.repayAmount` from both borrower principal and `totalBorrows` before/without learning the actual amount transferred in. Because `doTransferIn` only signals success or failure, short transfers are still accounted as full repayments.
impact: A borrower using a taxed/deflationary underlying can extinguish more debt than they actually repay, then withdraw collateral or leave the market with bad debt that honest suppliers absorb.
paths: repayBorrow/repayBorrowBehalf -> repayBorrowFresh sets `vars.repayAmount` -> debt and `totalBorrows` are reduced by that nominal amount -> `doTransferIn(payer, vars.repayAmount)` delivers less cash than was credited

title: Legacy liquidation over-seizes collateral from the requested repay amount
claim: The legacy liquidation flow computes `seizeTokens` from the caller-supplied `repayAmount` before repayment occurs, and the same legacy repay path cannot distinguish nominal from actual transferred value. On fee-on-transfer borrow assets, collateral is therefore priced from an amount the market never actually received.
impact: A liquidator can repay less value than assumed yet seize full collateral, directly stealing borrower collateral and worsening protocol insolvency.
paths: liquidateBorrow -> liquidateBorrowFresh computes `seizeTokens` from `repayAmount` -> `repayBorrowFresh` accounts full `repayAmount` even if less cash arrives -> collateral seizure executes using inflated `seizeTokens`

title: Missing `maxAssets` enforcement allows asset-list bloat to gas-grief liquidity checks
claim: `maxAssets` is documented as a cap on how many markets an account can join, but neither `enterMarkets` nor `addToMarketInternal` enforces it. As a result, `accountAssets[account]` can grow without bound, and every borrow, redeem, transfer, and non-deprecated liquidation liquidity check must iterate the entire array in `getHypotheticalAccountLiquidityInternal`.
impact: If enough markets are listed, an attacker can deliberately bloat their asset list with dust positions until liquidity checks become prohibitively expensive, which can block further risk checks and make unhealthy positions impractical to liquidate, leading to permissionless bad-debt accumulation.
paths: user enters many markets via `enterMarkets` or auto-entry on first borrow -> `accountAssets[user]` grows unbounded -> liquidation/borrow/redeem/transfer checks iterate all entries in `getHypotheticalAccountLiquidityInternal` -> calls can run out of practical gas

title: `fixBadAccruals` records COMP debt that future claims never honor
claim: `fixBadAccruals` records overpaid rewards in `compReceivable`, but later COMP claims only look at `compAccrued` and `grantCompInternal`. No claim path offsets, blocks, or collects against `compReceivable` before sending new COMP.
impact: Users marked as owing COMP can continue claiming newly accrued rewards normally, so mistaken COMP distributions remain unrecoverable on-chain and the intended remediation does not actually restore protocol funds.
paths: admin calls `fixBadAccruals` -> `compReceivable[user]` increases -> user later calls `claimComp` -> `grantCompInternal` transfers full newly accrued COMP without netting the receivable
